issues.criterion: verification[11] ("STRONGER (the real gate, replacing the vacuous literal grep above): NIZAM.json's 'modules' array contains exactly one well-formed object with path=='ecosystem', a non-empty purpose, and key_documents CONTAINING EXACTLY 'ecosystem/README.md'") -- the contract's own design_notes labels this entry (plus its verification[12] anti-vacuity companion) as "the actual, structurally-sound, anti-vacuity-proven check ... the blocking requirement" that supersedes the known-false-pass verification[10].
issues.evidence: The entry's acceptance_test TEXT claims 'exactly', but its actual command only tests set MEMBERSHIP: `'ecosystem/README.md' in mods[0].get('key_documents',[])`. It does NOT assert the list contains ONLY that one path. Built a scratch NIZAM.json copy (never touching the real, contracted NIZAM.json) whose 'ecosystem' module entry is `{"path":"ecosystem","purpose":"...","key_documents":["ecosystem/README.md","ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md"]}` (the second path does not exist on disk at HEAD) and re-ran verification[11]'s exact python logic against it (path substituted, logic byte-identical): it PASSES (exit 0), even though the module lists a second, dangling protocol doc the contract explicitly forbids (design_notes.design_constraint_no_dangling_references: 'NIZAM.json's new ecosystem module entry's key_documents lists ONLY ecosystem/README.md'). Confirmed with tools/validate.sh's real C4 check (`bash tools/validate.sh --target <scratch-copy>.json`) that C4 DOES catch this particular dangling-path variant today (FAIL: "indexed path(s) do not resolve on disk: ['ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md']") -- so verification[10]/[13]'s real C4 runs provide a partial, incidental backstop for THIS sub-case. However, this backstop is defeated the moment the extra path resolves on disk: feature 033 (dependency 031, running CONCURRENTLY in this same working tree per this contract's own concurrency_fence framing) is authoring exactly `ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md`. Simulated that landing (`touch ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md`, reverted immediately after the probe) and re-built the same scratch NIZAM.json variant: verification[11]'s logic STILL PASSES (membership-only), and this time `tools/validate.sh --target` (C4) ALSO PASSES, since the extra path now resolves on disk. So once 033 lands its file in the same working tree -- a scenario the contract itself explicitly anticipates and tolerates for OTHER surfaces (non_goals allow-lists `ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md` in the scope guard precisely because it may appear) -- NONE of the 17 verification entries would catch a 032 implementation that (by copy-paste, misunderstanding of 'navigates the module's protocol documents', or accidental over-eager indexing) lists a second, out-of-contract key_document alongside 'ecosystem/README.md'. This is not a hypothetical: it is the concrete, most-likely-to-occur failure mode this contract's own design_notes was written to prevent, and the specific check the contract names as 'the blocking requirement' does not enforce the 'exactly' semantics its own acceptance_test text claims.
issues.required_fix: Tighten verification[11]'s command to assert exact list equality rather than membership, e.g. replace `'ecosystem/README.md' in mods[0].get('key_documents',[])` with `mods[0].get('key_documents')==['ecosystem/README.md']`. Re-run the same scratch-fixture probe (extra-but-dangling path, and extra-but-existing path once 033's file is simulated as present) to confirm both variants now FAIL the tightened check, independent of whether C4 also happens to catch the dangling sub-case.

issues.criterion: verification[5] ("frontmatter version is bumped PAST the pre-existing stub's 0.1.0") -- the acceptance_test text asserts directional progression ('past'), not mere difference.
issues.evidence: The command's actual gate is `v is not None and v!='0.1.0' and any(... e.get('version')==v ...)` -- a not-equal test, not a greater-than/semver-ordering test. Constructed an in-memory frontmatter fixture with `version: 0.0.1` and a matching `change_log` entry for `0.0.1` (never written to any repo file) and evaluated the identical check logic against it directly: it evaluates to PASS, even though 0.0.1 is a semver DOWNGRADE relative to 0.1.0, not a bump 'past' it. A no-op-adjacent or careless implementation that accidentally decremented the version while adding a change_log entry would satisfy this criterion despite violating its own stated text.
issues.required_fix: Either (a) tighten the check to a real semver comparison (e.g. parse MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH tuples and assert the new tuple is strictly greater than (0,1,0)), or (b) soften the acceptance_test's prose to 'version differs from 0.1.0' if a directional guarantee is not actually intended. Low practical risk (no rational implementation benefits from downgrading), so this is not blocking on its own, but is a real mismatch between stated criterion and enforced check.

unsupported_claims: scope.files_modify[0].purpose (ecosystem/README.md) explicitly commits to 'a Shipped/Planned annotation per file's actual on-disk existence at authoring time' for each of the 9 numbered protocol documents in the module-navigation table -- reviewed all 17 verification entries and none of them checks for the literal words 'Shipped'/'Planned' (or any equivalent existence-annotation marker) anywhere in ecosystem/README.md. verification[7] only checks that each bare filename substring appears somewhere in the lowercased document text; a table listing all 9 filenames with no Shipped/Planned column at all would still satisfy verification[7] and every other entry. Non-blocking (the official feature_list_005.json acceptance tests for feature 032 do not themselves require this annotation, and it is a documentation-quality nicety rather than a load-bearing correctness gate), but it is a claim the contract's own scope text makes with zero corresponding verification.
observations: All 17 verification commands were extracted programmatically (not retyped) from .agent/contracts/032.json and re-run verbatim from the repository root against the live, uncommitted working tree. Results: verification[1] (4-keyword lifecycle), [2] (10-stage lifecycle), [5] (version bump), and [7] (module navigation) correctly FAIL pre-implementation (content not yet authored) -- these are the load-bearing content checks this contract exists to gate. verification[11] (structural NIZAM.json module entry) correctly FAILS pre-implementation (module not yet indexed). All other entries -- [3],[4],[6],[8] (anti-vacuity companions), [9] (dangling-reference regression guard), [10] (known false-pass, see below), [12] (anti-vacuity for [11]), [13] (validate.sh SUMMARY), [14] (scope guard), [15] (.agent immutability guard), [16]/[17] (non-goal byte-identical checks) -- correctly PASS pre-implementation, exactly as expected for regression-style/anti-vacuity/scope-guard entries that must hold both before and after implementation.

verification[10] (the official `grep -q 'ecosystem' NIZAM.json && bash tools/validate.sh --target NIZAM.json` check, design_notes-labelled a known false-pass) independently reproduced: `git show HEAD:NIZAM.json | grep -n ecosystem` returns exactly 3 pre-existing prose hits (registry/README.md purpose text, standard/cross_repo_governance.md summary, methodology/08's summary) and zero module entries -- confirming the grep half is vacuous at HEAD, matching design_notes' claim precisely, not merely trusted from it. Independently confirmed `grep -n ecosystem tools/validate.sh` returns no match (exit 1), corroborating design_notes' claim that build_shipped_md_set/build_payload_md_set never sweep ecosystem/ -- meaning C1/C2/C3/C5/C7/C8/C9/C10 genuinely do not apply to ecosystem/README.md today, and only C4 (via NIZAM.json's key_documents walk) and this contract's own bespoke checks enforce anything about the ecosystem module's content.

Structural NIZAM.json check ([11]) and C4 (via tools/validate.sh --target) mutation-probed with five disposable scratch NIZAM.json copies (built under the scratchpad, never touching the real, contracted NIZAM.json; all removed after use): (a) a well-formed single-entry module -> both PASS; (b) key_documents listing README.md plus a currently-nonexistent dangling doc -> [11]-logic PASSES (the defect reported as the high-severity issue above), C4 correctly FAILS; (c) key_documents present but omitting 'ecosystem/README.md' entirely -> [11]-logic correctly FAILS, C4 correctly FAILS (path does not resolve); (d) module entry missing the 'key_documents' key altogether -> [11]-logic correctly FAILS, C4 correctly FAILS via registry/nizam-index.schema.json's `required: key_documents`; (e) key_documents present but an empty list -> [11]-logic correctly FAILS, C4 correctly FAILS via the schema's `minItems: 1`. A sixth probe simulated feature 033 having already landed `ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md` in the working tree (via a reverted `touch`) with that path ALSO listed in key_documents alongside README.md: both [11]-logic AND C4 now PASS, concretely demonstrating the high-severity gap is not merely a nonexistent-path edge case but a realistic near-term failure mode given this contract's own stated concurrency with feature 033.

Regression-guard check ([9], the design_constraint_no_dangling_references enforcer) mutation-probed with four disposable README.md fixtures (scratchpad only, never touching the real ecosystem/README.md): a fixture containing the directory-qualified token `ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md` in its body correctly FAILS ([9]'s logic extracts the token via the C9-shaped regex and finds it does not resolve on disk); an otherwise-identical fixture referencing the SAME document by its BARE filename `01_clean_state_preflight.md` (no '/' prefix) correctly PASSES (the regex never extracts a non-'/'-qualified token, so zero candidate tokens are found) -- confirming [9] genuinely discriminates the directory-qualified-vs-bare-filename convention design_notes relies on, and that the contract's planned bare-filename authoring approach is the correct way to avoid tripping it.

10-stage lifecycle check ([2]) mutation-probed with two disposable README fixtures: one naming 9 of the 10 canonical stages (omitting 'repeat') correctly FAILS; an otherwise-identical fixture naming all 10 correctly PASSES -- confirming [2] discriminates a near-miss from a complete lifecycle, not merely 'stub vs. non-stub'.

Scope guard ([14]), .agent immutability guard ([15]), and non-goal schema/tools byte-identical check ([16]) each independently confirmed load-bearing via disposable, fully-reverted positive-control probes on the LIVE tree (not scratch copies, since these guards must be tested against the real repo root): (a) a throwaway untracked file outside the allow-list correctly trips [14] (`vlib_scope_guard: out-of-scope changed path: ...`), removed afterward, `git status --porcelain` confirms no residue; (b) appending a trailing newline to the historical `.agent/qa/031-contract-review.json` correctly trips [15] (`immutable audit record(s) touched: M .agent/qa/031-contract-review.json`), restored from a scratch backup, `git diff --quiet -- .agent/qa/031-contract-review.json` confirms byte-identical restoration; (c) appending a byte to `schema/frontmatter.schema.json` correctly trips [16] (`git diff --quiet HEAD -- schema/ tools/` fails), restored from a scratch backup, confirmed byte-identical afterward.

Contract 032.json is schema-valid: `python3 -c "jsonschema.validate(json.load(open('.agent/contracts/032.json')), json.load(open('schema/contract.schema.json')))"` exits 0, re-run independently as part of this review.

Official feature_list_005.json feature-032 acceptance_tests (lifecycle-defines-and-navigates, NIZAM.json-indexes-module, the 4-keyword grep, the frontmatter-schema check, the literal C4 grep-check, and the validate.sh SUMMARY check) are all represented, at least as strongly, among the 17 verification entries -- no missing_acceptance_coverage found against the official spec text.

Repository left byte-for-byte unmodified by this review: `git status --porcelain --untracked-files=all` before and after this review shows the identical set of untracked paths (`.agent/contracts/032.json`, `.agent/contracts/033.json`, `.agent/qa/033-contract-review.json` -- the latter appeared mid-review, consistent with feature 033 running concurrently as the task framing warned -- and the pre-existing `v0.6.0-release-notes.md`); no tracked file shows as modified. All scratch fixtures/probes were built exclusively under the session scratchpad or as disposable copies with a `__scratch_probe_*` / `__bogus_*` naming convention, and every live-tree probe (the `ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md` touch/rm, the qa-file append/restore, the schema-file append/restore, the bogus untracked file) was reverted within the same command and independently re-confirmed byte-identical or absent afterward.

032 does not touch anything belonging to the concurrently-executing feature 033 (ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md, .agent/contracts/033.json, .agent/qa/033*.json/.agent/evidence/033*), and .agent/run_state.json was not read for mutation and was not edited by this review, per instructions.

final_verdict.summary: REJECTED at revision 0. Sixteen of the seventeen verification entries are sound, non-vacuous, and independently re-confirmed load-bearing via reverted mutation probes against disposable scratch fixtures and, for the repo-wide guards, reverted live-tree probes: the content checks (lifecycle 4-keyword/10-stage, version bump, module navigation, structural NIZAM.json presence) correctly FAIL pre-implementation and their anti-vacuity companions correctly confirm HEAD does not already satisfy them; the dangling-reference regression guard genuinely discriminates directory-qualified from bare-filename references; the scope guard, .agent immutability guard, and non-goal byte-identical checks are all genuinely load-bearing. However, verification[11] -- the entry the contract's own design_notes names as 'the real gate ... the blocking requirement' replacing the known-false-pass verification[10] -- does NOT enforce the 'exactly' semantics its own acceptance_test text claims: it checks list membership, not list equality. A scratch-fixture probe (never touching the real, contracted NIZAM.json) proves a module entry listing 'ecosystem/README.md' PLUS a second, out-of-contract key_document still passes verification[11]; a second probe simulating the concurrently-executing feature 033 having already landed ecosystem/01_clean_state_preflight.md in the same working tree (a scenario this contract's own non_goals/concurrency framing explicitly anticipates) shows that BOTH verification[11] and the incidental C4 backstop (verification[10]/[13]) pass in that case too -- meaning this is a realistic, non-hypothetical near-term failure mode with zero verification coverage today, not a manufactured edge case. This must be fixed (tighten verification[11] to exact-list equality) before implementation begins, since it is the specific check the contract relies on to prevent the exact defect class (dangling/extraneous key_documents entries) its design_notes spend the most words worrying about. A low-severity, non-blocking-on-its-own mismatch was also found in verification[5] (the version-bump check enforces 'not equal to 0.1.0', not 'greater than 0.1.0' as its text claims), and a non-blocking unsupported claim (the contract's own Shipped/Planned annotation commitment has no corresponding verification entry). Required before re-review: tighten verification[11]'s key_documents check to exact-list equality and re-probe both the dangling and the existing-but-extra scratch-fixture scenarios to confirm both now fail. Recommended, not required: tighten or soften verification[5]'s version-bump language/logic; consider adding a lightweight Shipped/Planned-annotation check for verification[7] if that documentation-quality commitment is meant to be enforced.
